name: Gordaliza del Pino from Sahagún
name_original: Gordaliza del Pino desde Sahagún – circular por el Burgo Ranero
description: This moderate 35.9-mile road cycling route from Sahagún takes you through rural Castilla y León via Burgo Ranero, with 767 feet of gain.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Sahagún are primarily characterized by the flat plains of Tierra de Campos, offering expansive views of agricultural landscapes. The region is a significant point along the Camino de Santiago, providing a network of paths that are often suitable for road cycling. Routes frequently connect historical towns and villages, showcasing traditional Castilian rural life. The terrain is generally level, making it accessible for various fitness levels, though some routes may include unpaved sections. Best road cycling routes around Sahagún The most popular road cycling route is Gordaliza del Pino from Sahagún, a 35.9 miles (57.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 19 minutes to complete. This moderate route features an elevation gain of 767 feet (234 meters). Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Paredes de Nava from Villada – circular via the Puente de San Juan de Becerril, a moderate 55.8 miles (89.9 km) path. It offers an elevation gain of 904 feet (276 meters) and takes approximately 3 hours 28 minutes. Local road cyclists also love the Hike from Sahagún – circular via Mayorga and Grajal de Campos, a 48.8 miles (78.5 km) trail leading through historical towns, often completed in about 3 hours 8 minutes. This moderate route includes an elevation gain of 1044 feet (318 meters). Road cycling around Sahagún is defined by the vast plains of Tierra de Campos, the historic Camino de Santiago, and connections between traditional villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions. The routes in Sahagún are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from one review. More than 20 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sahagún's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Sahagún?
A: There are over 8 road cycling routes around Sahagún, offering a variety of experiences across the flat plains of Tierra de Campos. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Sahagún?
A: The terrain around Sahagún is primarily characterized by the flat plains of Tierra de Campos, offering expansive views of agricultural landscapes. While generally level and ideal for road cycling, some routes may include unpaved segments better suited for gravel or hybrid bikes. Routes often connect historical towns and villages, showcasing traditional Castilian rural life.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Sahagún?
A: Yes, Sahagún offers routes suitable for various fitness levels. For an easier ride, consider "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1243666014/we-leave-from-villada-circular-by-the-rio-sequillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;We leave from Villada — circular by the Río Sequillo&lt;/a&gt;," which is rated as easy. Many routes are moderate and accessible, making them suitable for those who prefer less strenuous rides, including families.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I see while cycling near Sahagún?
A: The region is rich in history. You can explore Sahagún's historical elements, such as the distinctive Mudéjar brickwork and the remaining structures of the Royal Monastery of San Benito, including the iconic "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4441511" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Benito Arch and Ruins of the Monastery of San Benito&lt;/a&gt;." Routes often pass through smaller, historical towns like Grajal de Campos and Moratinos, offering glimpses into traditional Spanish rural life. You might also encounter the "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/916337" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Geographical Center of the Camino Francés – Ermita de la Virgen del Puente&lt;/a&gt;".

Q: Can I cycle parts of the Camino de Santiago on a road bike in Sahagún?
A: Yes, Sahagún is a prominent stop along the Camino de Santiago (French Way), a network of pilgrimage routes. While many paths are suitable for road cycling, it's important to note that some segments of the Camino may be unpaved. The region's routes often follow quiet tracks and paths through classic Castilian landscapes, offering a unique way to experience this historic pilgrimage route.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Sahagún?
A: The flat plains of Tierra de Campos make Sahagún an appealing destination for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, making them ideal for exploring the region's routes. Summers can be hot, while winters can be cold, so plan accordingly.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Sahagún?
A: Yes, many routes around Sahagún are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170744927/hike-from-sahagun-circular-via-mayorga-and-grajal-de-campos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hike from Sahagún – circular via Mayorga and Grajal de Campos&lt;/a&gt;," which takes you through historical towns.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Sahagún?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the expansive views of the Castilian landscape, the tranquility of the flat plains, and the opportunity to connect with the historical Camino de Santiago and traditional villages.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes in Sahagún for experienced riders?
A: While the terrain is largely flat, some routes offer longer distances and moderate elevation gains for a more challenging experience. For example, "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153635205/mayorga-from-melgar-de-abajo-circular-by-santervas-de-campos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mayorga from Melgar de Abajo – circular by Santervás de Campos&lt;/a&gt;" is a moderate route spanning over 73 km with nearly 400 meters of elevation gain.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling in Sahagún?
A: Sahagún is a welcoming town for visitors, and parking options are generally available within the town center or near key attractions. It's advisable to check local signage for specific parking regulations and availability, especially during peak seasons.

Q: What is a popular moderate road cycling route starting from Sahagún?
A: A popular moderate route is "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046735843/gordaliza-del-pino-from-sahagun" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gordaliza del Pino from Sahagún&lt;/a&gt;." This trail covers approximately 57.7 km with an elevation gain of 234 meters, offering a good balance of distance and effort.

Q: Are there opportunities to explore other towns or villages on a road bike from Sahagún?
A: Absolutely. Many routes are designed to connect Sahagún with nearby historical towns and villages, allowing you to experience traditional Spanish rural life. For instance, the "&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1170744927/hike-from-sahagun-circular-via-mayorga-and-grajal-de-campos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hike from Sahagún – circular via Mayorga and Grajal de Campos&lt;/a&gt;" route takes you through several such settlements.
tips: Sahagún is a regional town in the province of Leon located between the Cea and Valderaduey rivers. A center for commerce already during the Roman occupation of the Iberian peninsula, it gained importance by being along the Jacobian pilgrim route towards Santiago.
Gordaliza del Pino is a small agricultural town in the Tierra de Sahagún region. The most remarkable monument of the town is the Church of Nuestra Señora de Arbas.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting and ending in Sahagún, you can typically find parking within the town itself. Look for public parking areas in Sahagún, which is a common starting point for many routes in the area.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for racebikes, suggesting mostly well-paved roads. The Castilla y León region is known for its rolling hills, so expect some gradual ascents and descents rather than steep climbs. It offers a moderate cycling experience through rural landscapes.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the rural charm of Castilla y León, with open views stretching for kilometers, green fields, and potentially wildflowers, especially in spring. The area is generally depopulated, offering a peaceful ride through agricultural landscapes and charming villages. You'll also pass by cultural highlights such as the Historic Monuments of Sahagún and the Church of Our Lady of Arbas.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Spring (April-June) and early autumn (September-October) are generally the best times. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and vibrant green landscapes with wildflowers, while autumn provides cooler weather and often clear skies, ideal for cycling. Summers can be quite hot in Castilla y León, so plan for early morning rides if cycling during July or August.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for cycling with dogs?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebikes on public roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, cycling with a dog on a leash alongside a racebike on roads can be challenging and potentially unsafe for both the cyclist and the dog, especially given the distance and potential for traffic. It's generally not recommended for this type of route.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths in the Castilla y León region of Spain.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through rural areas, Sahagún and Burgo Ranero are towns where you can find cafes, bars, and restaurants. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the stretches between these villages, as options might be limited in smaller hamlets like Gordaliza del Pino.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of the Camiño Francés (Rutas alternativas), the Camino Francés - 04 Burgos a León, and the Camino de Madrid - Melgar de Arriba -> Bercianos. These intersections are generally brief, as the route primarily follows its own distinct path.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: Rated as 'moderate', this route is best suited for cyclists with a reasonable level of fitness and some experience with road cycling. While it doesn't feature extreme climbs, the distance of nearly 58 km and rolling hills mean it might be challenging for absolute beginners. If you're new to road cycling, consider shorter routes first to build endurance.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: For this racebike tour, you should bring your bike in good working order, a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, sufficient water, snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers), and a fully charged phone. Sunscreen and sunglasses are also recommended, especially during sunnier months, as much of the route is exposed.

faqs.question: Are there public transport options to reach the starting point in Sahagún?
faqs.answer: Sahagún is a well-known town, particularly due to its connection with the Camino de Santiago. It has train and bus connections, making it accessible by public transport. Check local train and bus schedules for services to Sahagún from larger cities in Castilla y León.

waypoints.name: Church of Our Lady of Arbas
